caveats: a: The BLS proxy is much broader than the narrowed lens.
a: Manufacturing automation and vertical integration differ materially across firms.
rho: AI recommendations affecting listed equipment require trained human validation.
rho: Poorly structured manuals, serial records, and distributor data reduce realization.
e: The narrowed product family's share of the residual code is not published.
e: The frozen LMM count of 417 applies to the entire heterogeneous code, so applying this estimated share remains highly uncertain.
s5: No transfer source isolates the narrowed lens.
s5: The denominator is especially uncertain because the residual NAICS count spans unrelated businesses.
q: Public sources describe compliance and channel structure, not realized manufacturer pricing.
q: Environmental transitions in extinguishing agents can change competitive dynamics and costs.
d5: Regulated maintenance does not translate one-for-one into new-unit demand.
d5: Alternative evacuation policies and fixed suppression can reduce portable-unit requirements in some workplaces.
o: Operator displacement is distinct from reduced quantity due to alternative fire strategies, which belongs in d5.
o: Digital tags may reduce documentation labor without changing physical product demand.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is recurring business-to-business product and replacement-part supply through qualified distributors that sell, inspect, maintain, recharge, test, and support an installed base of regulated portable extinguishers; new facilities, replacements, hazard changes, and periodic service create repeat orders.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 339999 is a residual category combining unrelated consumer, seasonal, regulated, and equipment products, including candles, artificial trees, electronic cigarettes, hairpieces, amusement machines, umbrellas, and portable fire extinguishers. A common commercial and demand model is impossible, so the lens is narrowed to portable fire-extinguisher manufacturing and its distributor-supported installed base for coherence, not attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Artificial trees and ornaments, candles, lighters, amusement machines, electronic cigarettes, hairpieces, potpourri, tobacco pipes, umbrellas, nonmanufacturing fire-service contractors, captive plants, and platform-scale fire-equipment manufacturers outside the lower middle market.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers of approved portable fire extinguishers and closely related extinguisher parts supplied repeatedly through authorized fire-protection distributors and service organizations to commercial, industrial, healthcare, education, government, vehicle, and specialty customers.

a.bridge: BLS occupation data cover all miscellaneous manufacturing, not portable fire-extinguisher manufacturers within 339999. I map management, office, sales, engineering, technical documentation, distributor support, product selection, compliance, planning, procurement, and quality-record tasks into partial AI exposure while excluding cylinder fabrication, filling, valve assembly, pressure testing, labeling, warehousing, and material handling. Regulated fire equipment may have more engineering and compliance work than the broad group, so the proxy could understate exposure; highly automated volume production could make it overstate exposure.
caveats: The BLS proxy is much broader than the narrowed lens.
Manufacturing automation and vertical integration differ materially across firms.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted occupation and task study for U.S. portable-extinguisher manufacturers, including engineering, compliance, distributor support, production, and testing, would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: AI can assist product and hazard matching, distributor questions, manual and compliance retrieval, quote and order handling, quality-document drafting, purchasing, forecasting, and scheduling. Physical production and safety testing remain dominant operator-controlled workflows.

d5.bridge: OSHA regulations measure workplace placement, inspection, maintenance, and test obligations rather than manufacturer shipments, while Federal Reserve industrial production covers broad customer activity. I translate the regulated installed base, recurring replacement and genuine-parts needs, and modest industrial and construction activity into slight five-year real demand growth. The bridge may overstate shipments if longer product life and recharging satisfy more demand, or understate them if new battery and clean-agent hazards accelerate replacement.
caveats: Regulated maintenance does not translate one-for-one into new-unit demand.
Alternative evacuation policies and fixed suppression can reduce portable-unit requirements in some workplaces.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year U.S. shipment forecasts by extinguisher class and agent, installed-base retirement data, new-construction placements, recharge-versus-replacement ratios, and regulatory transition schedules would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Portable extinguishers remain a required physical safety layer across a broad commercial and industrial installed base. Monthly inspection, annual maintenance, periodic internal procedures and hydrostatic tests sustain parts and replacement demand; building cycles, agent regulation, and alternative suppression systems create uncertainty.

caveats: The narrowed product family's share of the residual code is not published.
The frozen LMM count of 417 applies to the entire heterogeneous code, so applying this estimated share remains highly uncertain.
e.change_evidence: A product-level classification of all 339999 firms matched to portable-extinguisher manufacturing, ownership, revenue mix, distributor recurrence, and normalized EBITDA would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: Portable extinguisher manufacturers can fit the repeat outsourced B2B lens, but they are only one illustrative product family inside a 3,012-establishment residual category. Eligibility is further reduced by corporate ownership, businesses outside the LMM band, and firms centered on service or distribution rather than manufacturing.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 339999 ($5,314,960,581) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 339999 ($14,224,295,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $331,013 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 339999); class <5: 1,626 firms, avg revenue/firm $458,236 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $89,906 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 565 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,502,552 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $294,801 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 393 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,011,109 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $590,780 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 344 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,776,686 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,918,186 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 73 firms, avg revenue/firm $36,068,904 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,076,719 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $132,943,537 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $26,083,522 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 417

caveats: Operator displacement is distinct from reduced quantity due to alternative fire strategies, which belongs in d5.
Digital tags may reduce documentation labor without changing physical product demand.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on fixed-system substitution, workplace evacuation-only policies, autonomous suppression, customer in-house production, and digital compliance that bypasses existing manufacturers would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Portable extinguishers are physical, approved pressure vessels requiring manufacture, filling, testing, labeling, distribution, maintenance parts, and accountable quality systems. AI can assist selection and documentation but cannot supply fire-suppression capacity as software.

q.bridge: Manufacturer and OSHA sources show approved and tested products, authorized distributor networks, ongoing maintenance, product support, and manufacturer-specific parts, but do not measure retained AI benefit. I infer meaningful retention from certification, reliability, technical support, and channel relationships, then discount for distributor bargaining, competitive bids, standardized ratings, and contract repricing. The proxy may overstate retention for common dry-chemical models and understate it for specialized clean-agent or hazard-specific products.
caveats: Public sources describe compliance and channel structure, not realized manufacturer pricing.
Environmental transitions in extinguishing agents can change competitive dynamics and costs.
q.change_evidence: Gross-margin bridges after measured administrative or support savings, segmented by standard portable units, specialty agents, parts, and distributor contracts, would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Safety approvals, tested configurations, genuine parts, reputation, and distributor training create switching friction. Standard units remain comparable across established brands, while large distributors and end customers can share savings through procurement.

caveats: AI recommendations affecting listed equipment require trained human validation.
Poorly structured manuals, serial records, and distributor data reduce realization.
rho.change_evidence: Measured pilots covering distributor-case resolution, quote and order cycle time, parts lookup, compliance-document preparation, production planning, and quality deviations would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Within five years, operators can implement AI in distributor support, application and model selection, standards search, manuals, parts matching, order entry, planning, purchasing, and quality-document workflows. Safety-critical approvals, listed configurations, test evidence, legacy systems, and the cost of incorrect advice constrain implementation.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell measures broad manufacturing transactions and Census owner-characteristics data are economy-wide rather than control transfers of portable-extinguisher manufacturers. I use them to infer a moderate five-year succession rate, then reduce it for a small, approval-intensive product niche with visible corporate ownership and strategic buyers. The broad transaction pool likely overstates qualifying transfer frequency.
caveats: No transfer source isolates the narrowed lens.
The denominator is especially uncertain because the residual NAICS count spans unrelated businesses.
s5.change_evidence: A complete U.S. manufacturer ownership map with owner age, UL or FM listings, corporate affiliation, and recent fire-equipment control transactions would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Independent manufacturers can face succession and attract strategic fire-protection buyers, but certification, liability, scale, and customer-channel concentration restrict the transferable population.

principal_driver: A regulated installed base and manufacturer-specific product knowledge create recurring distributor support, parts, documentation, and replacement workflows.
principal_weakness: Portable extinguisher manufacturers are an unmeasured small subset of a 3,012-establishment residual code, making the actionable target universe highly uncertain.
